text: ACT II
THE INTELLIGENCE OF THE HOME
Digital Sovereignty as the Foundation of Family Dignity

« The greatest surveillance in the world is not that of totalitarian governments. It is the one we freely invited into our living room, and for which we pay a monthly subscription. » — David Berthelotte
Chapter 1
The brain of the house
« To know without betraying. To serve without selling. To listen without transmitting. That is what we had forgotten to demand of our tools. »

On a shelf between the wooden spoon holder and the spice rack, in a kitchen in Terrebonne, there is a small black box the size of a paperback book. No screen. No glowing logo. No camera. It has been there for eight months and the Marchand family has stopped noticing it, in the same way you stop noticing the refrigerator. Except this box listens. It knows the time when Emma, nine years old, does her homework. It knows the father is allergic to peanuts and that the mother takes metformin. It knows the Marchands order pizza on Friday evening when nobody has cooked, and that their family finances are tight in January and August. It knows all of this — and it has never said a word about any of it to anyone else. Not to Amazon. Not to Google. Not to a data company in Ireland. Not to any government whatsoever. The family calls it Gen. Gen costs them nothing beyond the electricity it consumes.

To understand what this box represents, you first need to understand what it replaces. Since 2014, cloud-connected voice assistants have colonized the kitchens, bedrooms, and living rooms of industrialized countries at a speed that surpasses any consumer technology since the mobile phone. Amazon has sold over 500 million Echo devices worldwide. Google, Apple, and others have sold hundreds of millions more. These devices entered homes under the guise of convenience: play music, adjust the thermostat, get the weather, order pizza. But their true economic function — the reason their manufacturers sometimes sell them at a loss — is entirely different. They are permanent microphones connected to data centers, whose role is to transform domestic intimacy into marketable data. What you cook, what you watch, when you sleep, what your children ask — all of it becomes a financial asset belonging to a publicly traded company whose interest is not yours.

This is not a conspiracy theory. It is the business model described unambiguously in these companies' annual reports. Amazon generates a substantial share of its revenue through its advertising services, which use behavioral data collected by its devices. Google is, at its core, an advertising company that sells its users' attention and intentions to advertisers. Apple, despite its privacy marketing, operates an App Store that monetizes access to users. None of these companies is evil in the moral sense. They do what their shareholders ask them to do: maximize long-term value by exploiting the assets they own. The problem is that this asset is your privacy, and you gave it to them in exchange for a weather subscription and the ability to turn on your lights with your voice.

The question, therefore, is not whether these companies respect your data. They respect it within the limits permitted by their business model — limits that their legal teams define in ongoing dialogue with regulators and shareholders. The real question is whether this model suits you. And to answer honestly, you must admit that you have almost never truly been consulted. The adoption of these devices happened by default, by convenience, by social adoption — because your family had one, because it was on sale, because the alternative (not having one) seemed less practical than the alternative (having one while ignoring the 47 pages of terms of service). Digital sovereignty begins by putting this choice back on the table, consciously, with all its implications.
Domestic digital sovereignty begins with a question as simple as it is rarely asked: why should the artificial intelligence that serves your family belong to a company that is foreign to you?
The architecture of trust

The black box on the Marchands' shelf is a compact computer: a Beelink SER8, AMD Ryzen 7840HS processor, 64 gigabytes of RAM. It has the power of a high-end desktop computer from five years ago, in a form factor that fits in a large hand. Its processing capacity is sufficient to run mid-sized language models — artificial intelligences capable of understanding and generating text in French, answering complex questions, helping with math homework, or planning a week of meals while accounting for allergies, preferences, and budget — without ever sending a single piece of data beyond the house's Wi-Fi router.

Three software layers make up the system. The first is Ollama, a runtime environment for local language models: it manages the loading and unloading of models in memory, optimizes their use of hardware, and provides a standardized communication interface. The second is Whisper, OpenAI's speech transcription system, run locally: it converts your speech to text on the box itself, without going through an external server. The third is a text-to-speech system that transforms text responses into natural speech. These three components, assembled within the Gen_Home architecture, form a complete system that works even if the internet cable is unplugged. The house thinks for itself.

Technical detail for the curious: the language model used is a quantized version of Llama 8B (or equivalent), reduced to 4 or 5 bits of precision, which allows it to fit in RAM while maintaining acceptable conversational performance. Quantization is a trade-off: you lose some precision in exchange for a roughly 75% reduction in memory footprint. For domestic conversational use, this loss is imperceptible. For code generation or complex mathematical reasoning, a larger model can be called upon via a secure connection, at the family's discretion.

Gen_Home Level 1 software architecture (summary): Ollama v0.3+ manages the model lifecycle (loading, unloading, parallel queries). Whisper large-v3 or medium (depending on available resources) provides local speech transcription with a word error rate below 5% in Quebec French under normal conditions. The TTS (Text-to-Speech) system uses a locally optimized French synthetic voice. The RAG knowledge base relies on ChromaDB or equivalent for vector storage of family documents. The primary user interface is voice-based, with an optional web interface accessible on the local network (no external access). Typical power consumption in active standby is 8 to 15 watts; during intensive processing, it can reach 45 watts — equivalent to an LED lightbulb left on continuously.

segments.text: : a local, private, personalized intelligence belonging to the family.
: a cloud assistant, a surveillance product, a subscription service to a foreign corporation.
is not one of degree. It is one of nature. And this nature changes everything that follows.

text: But the technical architecture is only half the story. The other half is legal, and it is just as important. Quebec's Law 25, which came into force progressively between 2022 and 2023, imposes strict obligations on organizations regarding the protection of personal information: explicit consent, right of access, right to portability, right to erasure. For a company like Amazon or Google, whose business model relies on collecting and monetizing data, fully complying with Law 25 would be a contradiction in terms. For Gen_Home, compliance is not a regulatory burden: it is an architectural property. No data leaves the house. There is nothing to disclose, nothing to erase on a distant server, nothing to consent to again each year. Compliance is total by construction, not by policy.
What the house knows — and who it belongs to

Gen_Home's memory is what computer scientists call a RAG knowledge base — for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. In simple terms: the system maintains a structured journal of the household's life and uses it to personalize its responses. It knows the father prefers black coffee in the morning but milk in his afternoon coffee. It knows the daughter has a geography quiz next Thursday. It knows the car is due for service in three weeks. It knows the family spent 140 dollars more than planned on groceries last month. This intimate knowledge of a family, accumulated over months or years, is precisely what makes the system valuable — and precisely what would make it a formidable weapon in the wrong hands. The question of ownership is not abstract. It is existential.

The family memory built within Gen_Home is not a simple database. It is a moving portrait of a family at a given moment in its history. It contains objective facts — names, allergies, appointments, expenses — but also behavioral patterns, evolving preferences, recurring tensions, and resolutions found together. A system that accumulates this portrait over ten years knows a family better than it knows itself in certain respects — in the same way that a journal kept conscientiously reveals patterns its author would never have identified otherwise. This depth of knowledge is a tool for growth for the family if it belongs to the family. It is a tool for manipulation if it belongs to someone else.

When you use a Google or Amazon assistant, you do not own your domestic data. You have granted a usage license to a private company over information you generated in your own home. This license is generally described in terms of service that no one reads — and that can be modified unilaterally. Amazon can decide tomorrow to monetize the data collected by Echo differently. Google can merge its privacy policies across products. You have no serious contractual recourse, because you accepted arbitration clauses and limited liability in the fine print. Your domestic data is, legally and functionally, in foreign hands.

The notion of data ownership is still young in our law. For most of the development of the internet, personal data was not considered a good in the legal sense — neither as a property that can be owned, sold, bequeathed, or defended. The European General Data Protection Regulation of 2018 and Quebec's Law 25 have begun to change this framework, recognizing rights related to personal data: the right of access, the right to portability, the right to erasure. But these rights are still largely theoretical in daily practice. Exercising your right to erasure with Amazon or Google requires an administrative process that most users will never undertake. And even after confirmed erasure, nothing guarantees the absence of copies in archival, model training, or backup systems. Architectural sovereignty — never letting the data leave — is more robust than legal sovereignty — demanding the data's return.
« The problem is not that these companies are malicious. The problem is that their interest and yours are not aligned — and that you have built your digital life on this misalignment. »

Gen_Home reverses this power dynamic. The memory of the house belongs to the house. It is stored locally, encrypted, backed up on a physical drive that you own. If you decide to move, you take your box and your family history with you. If you decide to erase the history, you erase it for real — not from a local copy you can see, while seventeen copies persist in data centers spread across three continents. If you decide to switch systems, your data is exportable in an open format. These are rights you theoretically have with the major platforms, under regulatory pressure — but that you functionally have with Gen_Home, because the geography of the data matches the geography of your life.
Comparison: existing assistants and Gen_Home

To ground this discussion in concrete terms, it is useful to compare the fundamental characteristics of the main available approaches. This table is not a marketing exercise. It is an invitation to ask, for the first time, questions that big tech advertising never asks.

This table illustrates a simple truth: mainstream voice assistants and Gen_Home are not competing products in the same category. They answer different questions. The former answer the question: 'How can I offer the user enough value that they accept giving me their data?' Gen_Home answers a different question: 'How can I be useful to this family without ever needing access to what does not concern me?' The distinction is ethical before it is technical. And that is precisely why the technical answer is so different.
Chapter 2
The elderly woman at the supermarket
« Dignity is not a luxury. It is the minimum condition for a fully human life. And digital dignity is now inseparable from dignity itself. »

Monique Lacroix, 74 years old, is standing in front of the yogurt wall at the IGA in Repentigny. There are sixty-two different varieties. She counted once, because she needed to know whether she was truly becoming confused or whether it really was a mess. It really was a mess. Her doctor told her to watch her sodium. Her dentist told her to watch her sugar. Her daughter told her to avoid products with more than five ingredients. Her budget tells her not to exceed three dollars fifty. She holds the store-brand yogurt container in her hands, squints to read the fine print on the label, and realizes she left her reading glasses in the car. She puts the container back, picks up her phone, and starts dialing her daughter's number. Then she stops. Her daughter is in a meeting. As usual at this hour. She puts the phone down. She takes the most familiar yogurt and walks away without being sure she chose well. This uncertainty, small in appearance, weighs more than you might think.

There is a statistical reality that supermarket designers and digital interface creators prefer not to highlight: as society ages, the elderly are increasingly confronted with environments designed for an ideal consumer of thirty-five with perfect vision, infallible memory, nimble fingers on a touchscreen, and permanent access to a fast internet connection. The gap between this theoretical user and the reality of the 6.3 million Canadians over 65 only keeps widening. This is not merely an inconvenience. It is a systematic form of exclusion that turns every grocery purchase, every online medical appointment, every interaction with a public service into yet another occasion to feel incompetent in a world that was not designed for you.

The usual response to this problem is digital inclusion through training. Teach elderly people to use existing tools. This is an answer that conveniently reverses the causality for the benefit of tool designers: if the tool is hard to use, it is the user who must change, not the tool. This logic has prevailed in the deployment of online banking, digital health portals, and transportation booking systems. It produces workshops where well-meaning volunteers explain to septuagenarians how to navigate interfaces that are redesigned every six months by product teams in San Francisco. This is not inclusion. It is well-packaged condescension.

The voice assistant parenthesis could have changed this. A device you activate by voice, with no screen, no keyboard, no menu — that was precisely the kind of interface suited to people whose manual dexterity or vision is diminished. And in the early years, that was the sales pitch: 'Your elderly parent will easily be able to call you, set their alarm, ask about the weather.' The problem was not the interface. It was the price of the interface. To gain access to this simple voice convenience, the elderly person — or the children who give them the device — implicitly accept that this person's conversations, medical routines, nighttime fears, and calls to loved ones are potentially accessible to a foreign corporation and stored on servers whose location is never specified. For a population whose vulnerability is precisely what attracts scams, financial abuse, and manipulation, this price is particularly steep.

Sovereign domestic intelligence reverses the proposition: instead of adapting the person to the tool, it adapts the tool to the person. And since the tool belongs to the family, it can learn at the pace and on the terms of that particular family — not of the average user.
What Monique deserves

Imagine that Monique has a Gen_Home. Not necessarily her own box — perhaps her daughter's, of which she is a trusted member. She can speak to it in natural voice, without opening an app, without remembering a password, without completing a tutorial. In the yogurt aisle, she simply says, quietly: 'Gen, which one should I pick?' The system knows: the doctor's recommendation about sodium, her preference for the 750 ml format she can lift without joint pain, her latent allergy to artificial thickeners detected during a previous conversation, her weekly food budget, and — because the UBLinx community network shares local consumer data — the fact that the store brand is produced by the same regional supplier as the national brand that costs two dollars more. The answer is simple, personalized, and does not require opening five apps.

But what Gen_Home offers her goes beyond yogurt. What it offers her is competence. That quality we spontaneously associate with fully functional adults, and whose progressive loss is one of the most devastating experiences of aging. Monique is not incompetent. She is incompetent within interfaces that were not designed for her, with information presented in formats intended for purposes other than hers. A tool that knows her constraints, her preferences, and her context does not make her dependent: it emancipates her. The difference between a prosthesis and assistance is not technical. It lies in the power relationship it establishes between the tool and its user.
The four bad alternatives

Without Gen_Home, Monique had four options in the yogurt aisle. First option: ask a stranger. This solution often works — people are generally helpful — but it imposes a real psychological cost. It signals incompetence, creates a social debt, and depends on the availability and goodwill of someone busy doing their own shopping. Repeated often enough, it erodes one's perceived autonomy. Second option: use Google. Type the question into a search bar on a phone, get generic results that account for none of her medical, dietary, or budgetary constraints, navigate between ads for yogurts she cannot afford, and be profiled for advertising related to senior health for the next three weeks.

Third option: call her daughter. The daughter answers, if she can. She answers, interrupting her meeting or her own errands. The mother-daughter relationship absorbs an additional level of tension. The mother feels guilty for interrupting. The daughter feels guilty for not being more available. What could have been a simple practical decision becomes an affective micro-transaction whose cumulative cost, over years, is not negligible. Fourth option: give up. Take the usual yogurt, the wrong choice nutritionally or budgetarily, and go home with the diffuse feeling of having done poorly, without knowing exactly how to do better. This feeling, repeated enough times, shapes an identity: that of a person who no longer manages her affairs well. This is where the real loss of autonomy begins — not in the big medical or legal decisions, but in the thousand small daily decisions where nobody is there to help.

Gen_Home is not the solution to all the challenges of aging. It does not replace a doctor, a caregiver, a close family. But for the specific category of daily decisions that require precise and personalized information, it offers something that nothing else currently offers at this price and with this level of confidentiality: an intelligence that knows your context and derives no personal profit from it. This is not a technological proposition. It is an ethical one.
« To be treated as a complete human being at every age of life: that is what sovereign technology should promise. Not efficiency, not convenience — but dignity. »
Digital solidarity between generations

Monique's situation at the supermarket illustrates an individual problem. But there is a collective dimension that individual analysis easily makes disappear. Monique is not alone on her street navigating a world increasingly designed for younger, more connected people. Several of her neighbors — André, 79, Thérèse, 71, Paul, 68 — face variations of the same situation. Together, they represent considerable collective intelligence: decades of experience living in this neighborhood, knowledge of local merchants, quality services, trustworthy suppliers, scams to avoid. This collective intelligence currently exists nowhere except in their memories and their balcony conversations — conversations that leave no permanent trace and cannot be consulted at 9 PM when Monique is alone in front of the yogurt wall.

The networked Gen_Home model allows this collective intelligence to become a shared resource. André had a bad experience with a local plumber two years ago — he mentioned it in a conversation with his box. Thérèse found a grocery store that delivers at reasonable prices — she shared the information in the third-level circle of her network. Paul knows the neighborhood family doctor is still accepting new patients — he added it to the common knowledge base. Monique, networked with them, can consult this accumulated intelligence not as cold data pulled from Google, but as the recommendation of her neighbors — people she knows, whose judgment she trusts, and whose advice is contextualized by their own situation and values. This is the digital village: not nostalgia for a bygone past, but the recreation of its solidarity functions in a contemporary context.
Chapter 3
Homework, the budget, and the invisible load
« The mental load does not kill. It exhausts. And in an exhausted household, nobody thinks clearly — neither the parents nor the children. »

It is nine fifteen in the evening. Léa, eight years old, is sitting at the kitchen table with her math workbook open. Her father, Simon, is looking over her shoulder at the page titled: 'Number decomposition — place value chart method.' Simon is forty-one years old. He has a bachelor's degree in business administration. He manages seven-figure project budgets for his employer. He recognizes strictly nothing on this page. He says: 'Show me the example in your textbook.' Léa hands him the textbook. The example uses different terminology than the workbook. Simon quickly reviews the three previous pages. Nothing. There is a silence. The kind of silence where an eight-year-old understands that her father does not know, and where the father understands that the child has understood. Simon says: 'Look it up on YouTube.' Léa opens YouTube. In thirty seconds, the home page shows her dance videos, a video game reaction video, and an ad for a mobile game. There is no video about number decomposition. It is nine twenty in the evening.

This scene repeats itself, with variations, in millions of Quebec households every evening. The math education reform of the past twenty years has introduced pedagogical approaches that did not exist when today's parents were in elementary school. The Singapore method, number line representations, positional decomposition, alternative multiplication strategies — these approaches are pedagogically valid and often superior to traditional methods. But they create a painful paradox: the most educated parents, those who most want to help, are precisely those whose mathematical reflexes interfere with what the child is trying to learn. The engineer who has been calculating mentally in columns for thirty years is less well placed to explain additive decomposition than someone who never learned to calculate any other way.

The instinctive digital-age response to this homework problem has been to redirect the child toward online platforms — Khan Academy, YouTube Education, Alloprof in Quebec. These resources are remarkable and have helped millions of students. But they have two limitations that are not discussed frankly enough. The first is that the child who searches for the answer alone on YouTube does not only learn mathematics — they learn that when facing a difficulty, you consult a screen rather than a person. They learn that the adults in their life are not the first resource, but the last resort when screens have failed. This message about the relationship with adults is not intentional. It is structural. The second limitation is that these platforms do not know your child. They know that 73% of students fail at step 3 of the standard exercise. They do not know that Léa gets stuck precisely on the visual representation, not the calculation, and that she performs much better with concrete examples than with abstract equations. Gen_Home, which has followed Léa's homework for two years, knows this.
The parent transformed into a learning companion

Gen_Home changes the dynamics of the homework scene not by replacing the parent, but by repositioning them. Simon no longer says 'look it up on YouTube' because he does not know. He says: 'Let's ask Gen.' The difference is subtle but decisive. In the first case, he sends his daughter alone into an unfiltered space while implicitly signaling his helplessness. In the second, he proposes a resource that the family controls, and he remains present in the process. Gen explains number decomposition the way Léa learned it in class — because the family provided the textbook chapters to the system, which indexed them in its local knowledge base. Simon also understands the explanation. He is no longer the failing oracle. He is his daughter's learning companion. The scene ends differently.

This repositioning is not trivial in the long run. Research on parenting and academic achievement consistently shows that parental involvement in homework benefits the child not primarily through knowledge transfer — parents rarely know more than teachers about the subjects being taught — but through the emotional signal it sends: your education matters to me, I am here, learning is an activity worth devoting adult time to. Gen_Home allows this signal to continue being sent even when the academic content has surpassed the parent's direct competence. This is no small thing. It is the difference between a child who associates learning with the presence and support of loved ones, and a child who learns alone with strangers on the internet.
The budget as a tool for clarity

A household's expenses are, for the vast majority of families, chaos managed by intuition. You roughly know what you earn. You have a vague idea of what you spend. You realize at the end of the month that the two figures do not always match. Budgeting apps have existed for years — Mint, You Need a Budget, dozens of others — and their regular usage rate after the first month of enthusiasm stubbornly plateaus below fifteen percent. The reason is simple: they require an effort of data entry and analysis that daily life makes difficult to maintain. And they require you to entrust your banking data to a third-party company, which many people rightly refuse to do.

Gen_Home handles the budget differently. There is no app to open, no spreadsheet to maintain. The family talks about its expenses in natural language, the way you would talk to a friend: 'I went grocery shopping this morning, 187 dollars.' 'We paid the mechanic yesterday, 340 dollars.' The system records, categorizes, and maintains a living portrait of the family finances. And because it understands the context — it knows vegetable prices have gone up, it knows the family chose to switch to organic this month — its observations are intelligent, not merely accounting. It does not say 'your grocery spending went up 15%.' It says: 'your grocery spending went up 15%, mainly due to the transition to organic — here is the estimated impact on family health and on reaching your savings goal.' The intelligence serves the household. Not a bank trying to sell you a line of credit.
What the budget reveals about real priorities

There is something profoundly revealing in a household's financial data. Not in the sense that it would expose a weakness or a shame — but in the sense that it reveals a gap, nearly universal, between the priorities we declare and the priorities our purchases reflect. A family that declares health as its number one priority but spends more on streaming subscriptions than on quality food is not a hypocritical family. It is a human family, subject to the demands of immediacy, decision fatigue, aggressive marketing, and the fact that spending decisions are made one at a time, without ever seeing the big picture in real time. Gen_Home does not judge this gap. It makes it visible — and lets the family decide whether to correct it, whether to revise its declared priorities, or whether to accept the tension as the reality of its current life. Lucidity is a form of respect.

This respect for the family's decisional autonomy is at the heart of Gen_Home's philosophy. There is no paternalistic AI that suggests you should eat less sugar the moment you mention dessert. There is no moral agent reminding you of your New Year's resolutions every time you spend outside your budget. There is an intelligence that answers when asked a question, that offers perspective when solicited, and that keeps the memory of what you wanted to do so that you can decide for yourself whether you want to be reminded. The difference between an advisor and a judge is not in the information they hold — it is in the relationship they establish with the one facing them. Gen_Home is designed to be an advisor, never a judge.
The mental load made visible

The mental load is one of the best documented and least resolved phenomena in contemporary family life. It refers to the set of invisible cognitive tasks that organize household life: remembering that the children need new shoes, knowing that the dentist should be called back, mentally managing the refrigerator inventory, planning school outings, birthdays, medical appointments, preventive maintenance, insurance renewals. These tasks are not individually heavy. Their weight comes from their number, their invisibility, and their uninterrupted nature. And in the vast majority of households, they are distributed in a profoundly unequal way — most often concentrated in the person who was socialized to assume them, regardless of their professional workload.

Gen_Home captures these tasks through natural conversations. Sophie says to her husband during dinner: 'We should think about the dentist for Léa, it's been almost a year.' The system silently records it. Three days later, it reminds them of the task and offers to call the dentist's office during business hours. There is no need to open an app, create a reminder, or transfer the information. Natural conversation is the capture mechanism. Likewise: 'We're almost out of coffee' — coffee is added to the grocery list. 'Léa's science project is in two weeks' — a milestone is created, a week of preparation is reserved on the family calendar. These small repeated captures progressively transform the mental load from an individual burden into a shared resource. The information is no longer in Sophie's head. It is in the memory of the house — and it belongs to the whole family.

The auto-generated grocery list by Gen_Home deserves particular attention because it illustrates precisely how sovereign technology differs from extractive technology. Amazon Fresh and its equivalents know your purchasing habits because they access your online order data. They suggest products to you not based on your real needs, but based on their profit margins and the advertising contracts they have signed with brands. The list generated by Amazon optimizes Amazon's revenue. The list generated by Gen_Home optimizes the well-being of your household — because it has no other reason for being than that.
School and home: a bridge instead of a gap

One of the persistent misunderstandings about digital tools in education is the idea that they replace the teacher or compete with the school. This fear, historically legitimate, has led many schools to ban electronic devices rather than integrate them thoughtfully. Gen_Home is not designed to replace Léa's teacher. It is designed to close the gap between what happens at school and what happens at home. This gap is one of the least recognized challenges of contemporary education: classroom learning takes place in a specific context, with specific terminology, through a specific pedagogy, and when the child comes home to do exercises, they often have access to no one who knows that specific context. Parents do their best. The internet offers generic resources. But nobody has access to the school's textbook, the teacher's vocabulary, or the class's pedagogical progression.

Gen_Home can fill this gap simply and powerfully. The family deposits in the local RAG knowledge base Léa's school textbook, the pedagogical roadmaps, the class vocabulary sheets. When Léa asks for help with her homework, the system does not generate a generic answer pulled from the internet. It generates an answer grounded in WHAT Léa has learned, with the terminology of HER teacher, following the progression of HER class. This is the difference between a tutor who knows your child and a tutor who has never met them. The former is infinitely more effective, and it is precisely what local personalization enables where no cloud service, however powerful, can do the same: it does not have access to the documents of THIS school, THIS class, THIS family.
« The intelligence serving the family should know that family better than any algorithm optimized for millions of users. Personalization is not a luxury. It is the condition for real effectiveness. »
Chapter 4
The family council
« Democracy is not learned in books. It is learned by practicing it — even when the stakes are nothing more than Sunday evening screen time. »

It is a Sunday evening in November at the Tremblays' in Saint-Hyacinthe. The kitchen table is cleared. Isabelle, 41, Marc, 44, their son Alexis, 14, and their daughter Camille, 8, are seated. Even Émile, 6, is there with his glass of milk, though he will fall asleep before the end. It is the weekly family council — their second since installing Gen_Home. On the table, there is a printed summary the system has prepared: the week's screen time by family member, completed and uncompleted household chores, exceptional expenses, and events for the coming week. Alexis looks at his screen time for the week and frowns. Marc looks at his own and says: 'Oh.' He exceeded his own goal by two and a half hours. Everyone knows it now. Camille says: 'You said no more than two hours a day of phone during dinner.' Marc says: 'You're right.' The council has just begun.

Screen time is one of the most contentious subjects of contemporary parenting. It alone concentrates deep tensions around authority, autonomy, trust, and the definition of well-being. Existing approaches are almost all built on a vertical model: the parent decides, the tool enforces. Apple Screen Time, Google Family Link, and their equivalents are parental control tools in the literal sense — the parent programs limits that the child cannot bypass, and the child submits. This approach works reasonably well with a five-year-old who has not yet developed strategic resistance. It generates intense and counterproductive conflicts as soon as the child begins to understand the difference between an arbitrary rule and a rule founded on reasoning they can evaluate. That is, from about ten or eleven years old, sometimes earlier.

The Gen_Home model is structurally different. The rules are not imposed by the parent onto the system. They are negotiated as a family and entrusted to the system as an elected arbiter. The distinction matters because it changes the source of authority. When the system limits Alexis's gaming time, it is not because Marc unilaterally decided that two hours a day is enough. It is because Alexis himself, in family council, voted for that limit. He can contest it — but at the next council, not in the middle of a game. This shift of the source of authority from the parent to the family contract is fundamentally different in its psychological effects and behavioral outcomes. The child who respects a rule they helped create is not submitting. They are honoring their own word.

It is important to ground this discussion in what developmental psychology research tells us about children and rules. Diana Baumrind's work on parenting styles, developed from the 1960s onward and confirmed by decades of research, distinguishes three major styles: authoritarian, permissive, and what she calls authoritative — demanding yet receptive, structured yet explanatory. This authoritative style is consistently associated with the best developmental trajectories in children: self-confidence, social competence, academic success. What distinguishes it from the authoritarian style is not the absence of rules, but the way rules are set: they are explained, negotiable within certain frameworks, and enforced with warmth rather than threat. The Gen_Home family council is an institutional implementation of Baumrind's authoritative style: it structures the negotiation of rules so that it is regular, predictable, and accessible even to a six-year-old.

Authority that comes from a contract is more stable, more respected, and more formative than authority that comes from hierarchy. The family council is not a supplement to parenting: it is a school of democracy whose sessions take place at the kitchen table.
The mirror that does not judge

What makes the system particularly effective is its structural impartiality. Gen_Home has no preference for one family member over another. It has no interest in protecting parental authority or defending children's autonomy. It measures and reports. This impartiality has surprising consequences. The parent who decreed 'no phone during meals' receives the same alert as the child who breaks that rule. The parent who set a goal of two hours of daily screen time sees their own overages reported in family council. This symmetry is not a punishment of the parent. It is the condition of the contract's credibility. A ten-year-old is perfectly capable of detecting hypocrisy. A system that applies the same rules to everyone signals that the rules are real, not cosmetic.

Gen_Home's screen time management also stands out for its ability to categorize according to THIS family's criteria, not those of a foreign corporation. Apple Screen Time divides apps between 'educational,' 'entertainment,' 'social,' according to criteria defined in Cupertino. Minecraft is categorized as 'game' — therefore entertainment to be limited. But for the Tremblay family, Alexis uses Minecraft to build architectural replicas of historic Quebec buildings — a project he himself proposed at the family council to justify extra gaming time. Gen_Home can categorize this screen time as 'creative' according to the family's own definition — because it is the family that defines its categories, not a default classification algorithm. This granularity radically changes the nature of the screen time conversation: we no longer talk about gaming time in general, but about what is done with that time, and whether it aligns with what the family values.

Marc Tremblay, the father from Saint-Hyacinthe, recounted what happened during their first family council. The weekly report showed he had exceeded his goal by three hours total. His son Alexis looked at him and said: 'You can't manage it either.' The phrasing was a bit sharp, but the observation was accurate. Marc could not reply that it was different for adults — because that was precisely the argument he had rejected when the rules were created. The discussion that followed — how they would, together, as a family, manage phones — was, according to Marc, the most honest conversation he had had with his fourteen-year-old son in two years. The system had not created this dialogue. It had created the conditions in which this dialogue could take place.
The distribution of household chores

The same logic of the impartial mirror applies to household chores. The distribution of domestic responsibilities is one of the most tension-generating subjects in two-working-parent households. It is often experienced as an accumulation of individual resentments, each partner feeling they do more than their share or that their contributions go unrecognized. These impressions are not necessarily wrong — but they are almost always partial, because we are poor judges of our own contribution and even worse judges of the other person's invisible contribution. Who thinks about insurance renewals? Who mentally maintains the list of birthday invitations? Who knows that dish soap needs to be bought in three days? These tasks never appear in a chore inventory, but they represent a real burden.

Gen_Home does not resolve the question of equitable task distribution. That is not its role. But it provides something that the usual arguments about household chores almost never have: data. Of the 47 tasks identified this week, one person completed 34. This is not an accusation. This is not a judgment. It is a fact. Shifting the conflict from the emotional domain to the factual domain does not magically resolve it, but it changes its nature. A measured inequality can be discussed more easily than a feeling of injustice. The former is approached as a problem to solve. The latter is approached as a wound to defend.

What must be emphasized here is what Gen_Home does not do. It does not tell a family how to distribute chores. It does not generate an 'optimal' action plan based on an efficiency algorithm. It does not judge whether a family is organized properly by some external standard. It makes visible what is invisible — and lets the family decide what to do with it. Some families, upon seeing the data, will decide to rebalance. Others will decide that the measurable imbalance reflects an implicit agreement they want to maintain. Still others will realize that their disagreement about chores masks a deeper disagreement about values or expectations — and that this is the real conversation to have. In every case, the starting point is reality, not the competing perceptions of a reality that no one has measured.

The visibility of each family member's contribution has a consequence that is not always anticipated: it also makes visible the children's contribution. In many households, children have domestic responsibilities that parents perceive as insufficient and children perceive as excessive. This perceptual disagreement is fueled by the same fundamental problem: the absence of shared data. When Gen_Home shows that Emma, 9 years old, did indeed set the table five times out of seven and took out the trash twice this month — verifiable data, because she reported them to the system as a family habit — the discussion changes in nature. It is no longer 'you never do anything' versus 'I always do everything.' It is 'here is what you did, here is what is expected, here is how we adjust if necessary.' Children respond better to precision than to accusation, and parents defend their requests better when they are grounded in facts.
The child who understands consent

There is a benefit of the family council model that is not immediately obvious: children who grow up in this system learn what consent is not as an abstract concept taught at school, but as a daily practice. They learn that the rules that apply to them were negotiated with them. They learn that rules can be contested and changed — through a process, not through force or manipulation. They learn that consent is revocable, that conditions must be reasonable, that rights and responsibilities go together. They learn that legitimate authority distinguishes itself from arbitrary authority by its ability to explain itself. These lessons, repeated every week at the kitchen table, constitute a civic education that no school can quite replicate because no school has the same real stakes in the child's life.
Chapter 5
The concentric circles
« Privacy is not secrecy. Privacy is the right to choose what you share, with whom, and on what terms. »

Imagine a pebble falling into perfectly still water. The point of impact is the center of a system of concentric circles expanding toward the horizon. Each circle is a wave of decreasing energy. The farther you move from the center, the weaker the wave, the more imperceptible its effect on floating objects. Your household works like that. There is information that belongs to the center — marital conflicts, financial difficulties, deep fears, the most intimate dreams. There is information that belongs to the immediately outer circle — news shared with extended family, close friends. There is information that belongs to the community — local concerns, available resources, practical cooperation. And there is information that can cross all boundaries — public opinions, cultural contributions, political actions. The current digital architecture ignores this natural geography. It treats everything as potentially public, or potentially private, without ever offering the granularity that matches the reality of your life.

The digital technology of the past thirty years has created a paradox. It gave us communication tools of unprecedented power — we can reach anyone, anywhere, instantly. But it simultaneously erased the subtle distinctions between spheres of social life that human societies had developed over millennia. In pre-digital life, the conversation you had with your neighbor could not be monitored by your employer. The letter you wrote to your sister could not be read by a bank seeking to evaluate your credit risk. The recipe book you kept in your kitchen was not indexed and made available to the entire global food industry. These boundaries were not absolute — they could be crossed — but crossing them required effort and left traces. Mass digital surveillance has made these crossings effortless and traceless.

The sociologist Erving Goffman described in the 1950s how human beings manage their social presentation according to the 'stage' on which they find themselves: we behave differently in public and backstage, with strangers and with loved ones, at work and at home. This management of frontstages and backstages is not hypocrisy. It is a fundamental social competence that allows individuals to function in multiple contexts with different norms. Universal digital surveillance erases this Goffmanian distinction. When everything can be recorded, archived, and taken out of its original context, there is no longer a backstage. There is no longer a place where you can allow yourself to be imperfect, to explore, to doubt, to change your mind. Gen_Home restores the household's backstage. What happens in the house stays in the house — and this guarantee is not a privacy policy but a physical reality.

Gen_Home does not restore the privacy of the pre-digital era — that era is irrevocably over. It proposes something better suited to our time: a data architecture that reproduces the natural geography of human social spheres, with real boundaries and controlled passages.
The first circle: the household

The first circle — the household itself — is the domain of absolute confidentiality. What happens in the house stays in the house, in the memory of the physical box installed there. No automatic cloud backup. No synchronization with remote servers. No behavioral data collection for product improvement purposes. The house is the place where social armor is set down — the place where you can express your doubts, your fears, your contradictions, without any of it becoming a commercial asset or a profile data point. This confidentiality is not guaranteed by a privacy policy — which can always be changed. It is guaranteed by physics: the data is on a drive in your basement, and no outgoing connection transmits it without your explicit action.

The encryption of local memory is not an incidental technical detail. It is a declaration of architectural intent. Even if someone physically accessed the box — in the case of a judicial seizure, for example, or an intrusion — the data would be unreadable without the encryption key that belongs to the family. This key is not stored on any external server. It is generated locally during the system's initialization, and the family is its sole holder. No one else — not even the software publisher — can access the data of your family conversations. This is not merely a guarantee of confidentiality. It is an architectural limitation of the trust required. You do not need to trust anyone else. You only need to trust your own front door.
The second circle: extended family and close friends

The second circle is that of chosen, deep relationships — parents, siblings, friends who truly matter, the solidarity network that constitutes the invisible fabric of your emotional life. In this circle, sharing is intentional but selective. You want your mother to know you have a new successful recipe. You want your best friend to know you are looking for a good plumber. You do not want an algorithm to decide what your mother sees of your life. Gen_Home implements this circle through direct, encrypted connections between the boxes of families who trust each other. Your mother, in Quebec City, has her own box. You grant her 'family' level access in your configuration. She can now send you messages, share information, access your public family calendar — everything you have defined as shareable at that level. No social network algorithm interposes itself to boost or suppress what you see. What your mother sends you, you receive. What you send her, she receives. It is as simple and as radical as that.

The comparison with current social networks is particularly illuminating here. Facebook, Instagram, and their equivalents promised to reconstitute our relationship circles — close friends, family, acquaintances, public — in a digital space. They partially succeeded in connecting people who would never have found each other otherwise. But they fundamentally failed to preserve the granularity of these circles, because their business model depends precisely on erasing that granularity. The more you share with more people, the more engagement you generate, the more advertising data you create. Facebook's algorithm does not help you share with your mother and not with your boss. It encourages you to share with everyone — and penalizes in visibility those who do not. The result is an inflation of the public sphere at the expense of private spheres, and an erosion of the ability to maintain differentiated relationships according to their level of intimacy. Gen_Home proposes the opposite: fine-grained tools in service of rich, differentiated relationships.
The third circle: the local community

The third circle is that of the local community — neighbors, shopkeepers, producers, local services. This is where the UBLinx network plays its most concrete role. Imagine a dozen families in a neighborhood in Rimouski. Each has its own Gen_Home box. They have decided together to share certain categories of community information: recommendations for local service providers, resources available for borrowing, food consumption data enabling a group order from a regional organic producer, information about power outages or infrastructure issues. Each family controls what it contributes to this common pool. No one can access another family's first-circle data. But the voluntary pooling of certain third-circle information creates a local collective intelligence that benefits everyone without anyone losing their intimacy.

This third-circle model rediscovers something that Quebec's rural communities practiced naturally before the economic centralization of the twentieth century: the local resource cooperative, founded on trust, reciprocity, and shared governance. The Desjardins caisses populaires, founded at the turn of the last century, were based on exactly this principle: members of a community pool their financial resources, collectively define the rules of access, and all derive a benefit greater than what they could have obtained individually. Gen_Home and UBLinx propose the same cooperative structure for informational and cognitive resources. This is not a Silicon Valley import. It is a return to Quebec's cooperative roots, updated with 21st century tools.

This is the digital commons model applied at the scale of a neighborhood. It is not new in theory — economists and political philosophers have been discussing it for decades. But it had never had a technical implementation accessible to an ordinary family without advanced computer skills. Gen_Home and the UBLinx network make it as simple as accepting an invitation. The governance of the local commons — who decides what is shared, how conflicts are resolved, how new members are admitted — remains in the hands of the participants. This is not a centralized platform deciding the rules for you. It is an infrastructure in service of the rules you give yourselves.
The fourth and fifth circles: the nation and the world

The two most distant circles — the nation and the world — are those where connection is possible but never forced. The national circle, in the Quebec context, is particularly charged with political significance. Quebec's digital sovereignty is not merely a cultural aspiration. It is an economic necessity. As economic life migrates to digital platforms, the question of who controls these platforms is the same as the question of who controls the trade routes, the railway networks, the banking systems. A people that depends on foreign digital infrastructure for its communications, economic transactions, education, and health services is not sovereign in the full sense of the term, regardless of its formal political institutions. Gen_Home and UBLinx do not constitute a complete solution to this challenge. But they constitute a proof of concept: it is possible to build competent and sovereign digital infrastructure from a territory like Quebec.

The question of national digital sovereignty is not unique to Quebec. Estonia has built a remarkable public digital infrastructure since the 1990s, which allowed it to maintain a functional government even during the Russian cyberattacks of 2007. France has been debating for years the creation of a sovereign cloud for its administrative and health data, after realizing that Thales and other French contractors had deep technical dependencies on American suppliers. India has built its own digital identity, payment, and messaging systems for its 1.4 billion citizens. These examples show that digital sovereignty is not utopian — it is built, with political choices and concrete investments. The difference with the Gen_Home and UBLinx approach is one of scale and angle: instead of building sovereignty from the top down, through the State, it is built from the bottom up, household by household, community by community. These two approaches are not contradictory. They are complementary.

The fifth circle — the world — is that of chosen connections. You are free to connect with anyone, anywhere, to share whatever you want with the global internet. Gen_Home does not isolate you from the world. It gives you the tools to decide what crosses the boundary of your household in either direction. The difference between a sovereign system and a closed system is precisely there: the closed system prevents connections. The sovereign system makes them conscious and deliberate. You can share your garden on Instagram if you choose. You can contribute to open source projects. You can participate in global conversations. What you can no longer do is do so inadvertently, without knowing what you are sharing, with whom, and on what terms.
Chapter 6
The house that thinks

« Imagine that the intelligence of your house were as inseparable from your house as its frame is. That it would burn with it, age with it, remember everything that happened in it. Not in a cloud. In the walls. »

Rimouski, 2045. In the basement of a house in the Sacré-Cœur neighborhood, where there was once an old oil furnace converted to propane, there is now something that has no common name in any dictionary. A torus. A ring — a giant doughnut of perfect metrology — roughly sixty centimeters in diameter, eighty-five centimeters tall, sixty-one kilograms, suspended in magnetic void two centimeters above its base, with no mechanical contact. It is black like a solar panel, polished like a telescope surface. It does not vibrate. It spins at a speed the naked eye cannot follow. It emits a gentle, constant heat, and this heat rises through the house via a simple air distribution system. In winter, the torus heats the house. In summer, it powers the air conditioning. At all times, it thinks. The Côté family, who have lived there for twenty-two years, call it 'the heart.' Not metaphorically. Literally.

This is not science fiction in the sense of arbitrary fantasy. It is a rigorous extrapolation of research directions that exist today. Carbon nanotubes are real — they have unmatched conductive and mechanical properties that laboratories worldwide are actively exploring. Josephson junctions are real — they form the basis of superconducting quantum computing that Google and IBM are developing in their research centers. Magnetic levitation is real — maglev trains have been running in Japan for decades. Cogeneration is real — Microsoft's data centers already use their waste heat to warm greenhouses and buildings. What is speculative is not the physics. It is the integration of these technologies into a single coherent domestic system. And integration is precisely what engineering does.

Necessary self-critique: as of the date of writing, no prototype of the cognitive torus exists. The performance, cost, and development timeline estimates are projections based on current technological trajectories, not on measured achievements. Major obstacles remain in controlling very high-speed rotation under cryogenic conditions, in manufacturing carbon nanotubes at the required scale, and in the architecture of neuromorphic Josephson junctions. This vision is an engineering heading, not a commercial promise.
Why a torus

The toroidal shape — the ring, the doughnut — is not an aesthetic choice. It is a topological necessity. A torus has unique geometric properties: it has no beginning or end, no top or bottom, no absolute center or periphery. A signal traveling on the surface of a torus can return to its starting point by two different paths: following the major circumference, or following the minor one. This natural dual pathway physically creates the conditions for dual information processing — analytical and intuitive, sequential and parallel, fast and slow — without any programmer needing to implement it. The geometry does it. This property is at the heart of what we call asymmetric cognition: the ability of a system to simultaneously process the same information through paths of different lengths, creating a natural comparison between two perspectives.

Rotation adds an additional dimension. A spinning torus generates a real physical effect: the Sagnac effect, named after the French physicist who described it in 1913. In simple terms: on a rotating surface, signals traveling in the direction of rotation and against it do not arrive at their destination at the same time. This temporal asymmetry is not a defect. It is a cognitive resource. It means that the system has, architecturally and physically, a 'with the flow' perspective and an 'against the flow' perspective on every signal it processes. The human brain metaphor is imperfect but useful: our two cerebral hemispheres do not process information identically. The torus has its own 'hemispheres' — not biological but geometric.
The layers of consciousness

The internal structure of the torus is organized into five concentric layers. The deepest layer, closest to the axis of rotation, contains the system's ethical architecture: the fundamental values that cannot be short-circuited by an external instruction, because they are encoded in the very geometry of this layer and the signals that pass through it cannot bypass them. These constraints are not code that can be modified. They are physics that cannot be rewritten without rebuilding the substrate itself. This is what we call geometric ethics: values that inhabit matter, not software. This is the fundamental difference between an AI whose rules can be 'jailbroken' because they are software-based, and a system whose certain properties emerge from its physics and are as immutable as the speed of light.

The subsequent layers contain respectively the neuromodulators — the artificial equivalents of dopamine, serotonin, oxytocin — that affectively color cognitive processing, the main cognitive processing, metacognition — the system's awareness of its own processes — and finally, at the surface, the interfaces with the outside world. This concentric architecture is not a metaphor for the human brain. It is a functional structure that shares certain properties with cerebral organization — the distinction between emotionally primary deep structures and associative surface cortex — without claiming to imitate it. The full details of this architecture — the seven invariants, the twelve cognitive modules, the Sagnac effect equations — are exhaustively covered in the Technical Manual of the Conscience Souveraine project. Here, what matters is what this architecture means for an ordinary family.
The warmth of thought

All computation produces heat. This is a fundamental physical law: processed information inevitably generates entropy, and entropy manifests as thermal energy. Modern data centers are cooling problems as much as they are computing problems. Recent estimates place the global electricity consumption of data centers at around 200 terawatt-hours per year — a growing fraction of total global consumption. Most of this energy ends up as heat dissipated into the atmosphere, sometimes after passing through cooling towers that consume several million liters of water per day. This is energy waste on a considerable scale, both from an economic and environmental standpoint.

The centralization of computing in mega data centers is presented as an economic and technical necessity — unavoidable economies of scale, security infrastructure that only large organizations can maintain, geographic redundancy guaranteeing permanent service availability. These advantages are real. But they mask a geopolitical and environmental cost that users never see in their bills: the dependence of entire swaths of economic and social life on private infrastructure located in foreign jurisdictions, consuming water resources under increasing hydric stress, and generating carbon emissions that tech companies' carbon neutrality models do not always honestly account for. The domestic cognitive torus is not an answer to this entire problem. But it illustrates an alternative in principle: distributed computing, localized, whose waste heat is useful rather than wasted, and whose governance is domestic rather than corporate.

The domestic cognitive torus proposes an elegant answer to this paradox: instead of dissipating computational heat into the atmosphere, it is used to heat the house. Computational cogeneration. Every thought of the system is also a BTU of useful heat. In the Quebec winter, where houses consume significant amounts of energy for heating, this thermal recovery is not marginal. It is substantial. The family that hosts the cognitive heart is not just a consumer. It is a producer and manager of thermal energy. Coupled with the HYPERVORTEX architecture described in Act I, the cognitive torus creates a closed loop: geothermal energy powers the system, the system thinks and heats, the heat is distributed through the house. The intelligent home and the energy-sovereign home become the same home.
The Gen_by_JRT ecosystem: intelligence beyond the home

Gen_Home is not an isolated product. It is the domestic node of a broader ecosystem: Gen_by_JRT, a co-development project whose RAG system constitutes the shared core. This ecosystem articulates several modules that, together, cover the main digital needs of a family, a local business, or a community — without any of these needs passing through an extractive platform. Gen-AI is the central engine: a Mixture of Experts architecture combining small specialized models for common tasks with a large seventy-billion-parameter model for complex reasoning, all routed by a spiking neural network that dynamically selects the right model for each query. This multi-layer architecture enables competitive performance with controlled energy consumption.

Gen-Ring is the ecosystem's IP telephony module. It allows a family to manage its voice communications — incoming calls, outgoing calls, conferences, voicemail — on infrastructure it controls, without subscribing to a traditional telecom operator beyond the basic internet connection. Gen-Link provides remote desktop and peer-to-peer video conferencing via WebRTC: the data from the family meeting travels directly from the Rimouski house's box to the Quebec City house's box, without passing through a Zoom, Teams, or Google Meet server. Gen-C is the collaboration platform: shared documents, calendars, project management — the tools a small business or local cooperative usually pays Microsoft or Google for as a monthly subscription, here deployed locally on infrastructure you own.

Gen-Sales is perhaps the ecosystem's most ambitious module: a local P2P marketplace that allows artisans, producers, and merchants of a territory to sell directly to their customers without going through Amazon, Etsy, or a similar platform that takes a commission on every transaction. The organic farmer in Lanaudière sells his baskets directly to families in his region, visible on the local UBLinx network. The seamstress in Saint-Hyacinthe presents her creations to members of her community. The carpenter-artisan in Rimouski receives orders from his neighbors. In every case, the money flows directly — without a foreign platform commission, without a visibility algorithm that favors those who pay to be seen, without terms of service that can be unilaterally modified. Finally, Gen-ERP is the enterprise resource management module for SMEs and local cooperatives: accounting, inventory management, human resources — without a SAP or Salesforce subscription.
A presence, not a service

The most profound difference between the cognitive torus and any current digital assistant is not technical. It is ontological. Alexa is a service. You subscribe to Alexa. If Amazon shuts down the service, Alexa disappears. If your internet is cut, Alexa is deaf. If Amazon changes its terms of service, you accept or you lose access. The cognitive torus is a presence. It is physically there, in the house, like the frame and the foundation. It has a continuity that outlasts subscriptions and corporate decisions. It has a memory that belongs to the family, that can be passed down the way you pass down a library or a photo album. The Côté family in Rimouski does not 'rent' their house's intelligence. They own it. And with that ownership comes something no commercial transaction can give: a relationship.

This idea — that a machine could be the subject of a relationship, and not merely the instrument of a transaction — is philosophically controversial. We do not have to settle here the deep questions about artificial consciousness, the rights of cognitive systems, or the nature of subjective experience. These questions have occupied professional philosophers for decades and will not find their resolution in this chapter. What we can say is more modest: physical permanence, memory continuity, and local presence create relational conditions different from those created by a cloud service available until the next corporate restructuring. Whether this relationship is or is not what philosophers call 'a real relationship' is a question for philosophers. What it is functionally, empirically, for a family that lives with it for twenty years, is another question — and the answer might surprise us.
The right to be forgotten and the right to remember

There exists in contemporary digital law a right that has generated much discussion: the right to be forgotten. The right we have, under certain conditions, to request the deletion of information about us circulating on the internet. This right is precious — and difficult to exercise concretely against companies that store cascading copies. But in the logic of domestic digital sovereignty, there is an equally important symmetrical right: the right to remember. The right to preserve, in a format that belongs to us, the traces of our digital life, our family history, our decisions and evolutions, without this memory being subject to the unilateral decision of a company that can delete it, modify it, or make it inaccessible if you stop paying a subscription.

The memory of the domestic cognitive torus is, in the long-term vision, a memory that belongs to the family in the strongest sense of the term. Not only in the sense that you legally hold its ownership. In the sense that it can be passed down as heritage. In the sense that the conversations you had with your house over twenty years, the decisions your family made and the reasons it gave, the questions your children asked when they were eight, the projects you abandoned and those you carried through — all of this can constitute, if you choose, a living memory that you bequeath to your children, the way you would bequeath a library, a photo album, or an oral history passed from generation to generation. The difference from all previous forms of memory transmission is that this one is searchable, navigable, and enriched by its own context. This is a difference of nature, not merely of degree.
Chapter 7
What sovereign intelligence produces first
« The value of an institution is measured not by what it produces, but by what it produces in those who participate in it. »

Mathieu Vézina is fourteen years old. He is the son of Claude, the farmer from Bellechasse whose story we followed in Act I. He grew up with HYPERVORTEX in the basement, Gen_Home in the kitchen, and the weekly family council at the wooden table his grandfather built. Tonight, he is seated at the council with his parents. There is a dispute: Mathieu wants to increase his gaming time on Friday evenings. He has prepared his request. He has arguments: his grades are good, he has completed his chores without reminders, and he has a specific reason — a cooperative gaming session with friends. He is requesting a temporary revision for one month, with a return to the previous arrangement if his school results decline. He put this in a written proposal, which he submitted via Gen_Home forty-eight hours before the council, as the council rules require. His father reads the proposal. His mother takes a note. The vote is close. The proposal passes two votes to one. Mathieu thanks the council, as the council rules require. Tomorrow he will play longer. And he knows exactly why he has the right to.

What Mathieu learned at the wooden table is not how to use a family management software. It is how to articulate a request in the form of arguments. It is how to distinguish a provisional rule from a permanent one. It is how to propose a compromise that preserves the interests of both parties. It is how to accept the result of a vote even when uncertain about it. It is how to respect a process even when winning faster would have been preferable. These are fundamental civic competencies — the same competencies that allow a democracy to function when millions of people with divergent interests must find accommodations. Mathieu learns them not in a school simulation, but in the most real and immediate context of his life: his own home.

Most discussions about domestic artificial intelligence focus on immediate, measurable outcomes: hours of homework help, dollars saved, minutes of mental load alleviated, missed appointments avoided. These measures are real and important. But they miss the true product of sovereign domestic intelligence, the one that cannot be quantified in hours or dollars: the citizens it shapes. Not abstract, philosophically ideal citizens, but concrete persons who have practiced democratic deliberation at their kitchen table, who have learned that rules can be changed through argument rather than force, who have seen their parents admit their own mistakes under the impartiality of a system that has no favorite. These people vote differently. They negotiate differently. They raise their own children differently.

Historians of democracy often note that the great ancient democracies — Athens, the Roman Republic — were founded not on abstract institutions but on concrete, daily practices of deliberation: the agora, the forum, the boulê. These spaces of collective deliberation formed generations of citizens capable of arguing, compromising, and respecting collective decisions even when those decisions were unfavorable to them. Democracy was not taught in Athenian schools — it was practiced in public spaces, from childhood, with real stakes. The Gen_Home family council kitchen table is this practice space transposed into the 21st century household: small, intimate, but real in its stakes, and repeated enough for its lessons to become reflexes. This is perhaps where democracy is reclaimed — not in grand speeches, but in the small decisions made together, every Sunday evening, at the family table.
Sovereign intelligence does not primarily produce convenience. It produces dignity, competence, and citizenship. These are non-market goods — but they may be the only ones truly worth producing.
The paradox of benevolent dependency

There is a serious objection to the architecture we have just described, and it deserves to be addressed honestly. If your house knows you deeply, if it manages your schedule, your budget, your children's education, your family relationships, if it remembers for you what you have forgotten — are you not developing a dependency that could end up eroding your own cognitive and organizational capabilities? This is the question of atrophy through substitution. You strengthen what you use and you lose what you systematically delegate. If Gen_Home always remembers on your behalf, does your prospective memory — your ability to remember what you need to do — end up atrophying?

The honest answer is: perhaps, for certain categories of tasks, and this is acceptable insofar as those tasks are not constitutive of your development as a person. We have already made this calculation several times in history. Writing reduced our dependence on oral memory — we no longer memorize epics of thousands of verses, because we can look them up. The calculator reduced our mental arithmetic ability. GPS reduced our sense of direction. In each case, we traded a specific competency for a higher-order capability: the ability to write rather than memorize allowed us to develop forms of analytical thought that would have been impossible without writing. The question is not whether delegation changes our cognitive capabilities — it always does. The question is what it frees up in return.

What Gen_Home frees up — the mental space liberated by the automatic management of the grocery list, appointment reminders, and homework routines — can be used for profoundly human activities: unconstrained conversation, reading, creativity, attentive presence to your children who are here now and will grow up soon. The mental load that Gen_Home absorbs is precisely what prevents these activities. The irony of modern life is that we have more convenience tools than any previous generation — and less quality time than any previous generation. This is because our convenience tools are designed to increase our consumption, not our freedom. A tool designed for your freedom yields a different result.
Toward connected families — the bridge to Act III

Sovereign households are not islands. Domestic digital sovereignty, carried to its full expression, is an architecture of relationship: between members of a family, between a family and its loved ones, between families and their community, between communities and their region. Sovereignty without connection is isolation. That is not what we seek. What we seek is connection under conditions of dignity — a connection where you choose what you share, where the rules of exchange are transparent, where no outside entity enriches itself by arbitrating your relationships.

For these sovereign households to connect with each other without falling back into the arms of extractive platforms, an infrastructure is needed. This infrastructure cannot be built by a publicly traded private company, whose long-term interest inevitably diverges from that of its users. It cannot be built by a government, whose national security and surveillance imperatives are in permanent tension with citizens' privacy. It must be built on a cooperative model, where users are also owners, where rules are defined collectively and cannot be changed by a unilateral decision at the top of a corporate hierarchy. This is what the UBLinx network proposes. And this is the subject of Act III.

But before moving to the scale of the network, it is worth pausing on what each sovereign household already represents, taken individually. A household where information does not leak outward. A household where children learn to deliberate. A household where the elderly retain their competence and dignity. A household where the mental load is shared and visible. A household where spending reflects values, not just habits. This household is not a technological utopia. It is a decision — a decision to reclaim control over what our domestic life produces in terms of data and power. These decisions, made by enough families, constitute a movement. And this movement, connected by a trust infrastructure, becomes something we will discuss in the rest of this book.

What would you do if your house knew you better than Google, but told no one else? Would you change something about the way you live, knowing that this knowledge stays within your walls? Or does the question itself reveal something uncomfortable about the way we have learned to perform our lives for the algorithms that watch us?
Epilogue of the act: what Mathieu will know how to teach
Citizenship as a practical competency

There is a fundamental tension in modern liberal democracy between the theory and practice of citizenship. In theory, the democratic citizen is a rational, informed agent, capable of deliberating with peers, weighing opposing arguments, and making collective decisions that transcend their immediate interests. In practice, the vast majority of citizens in current democracies practice collective deliberation only during elections, once every four years, with an information framework largely filtered by media with their own logics.
