This gap between the theoretical civic competence required by democracy and the effective deliberation practice that daily life offers is one of the deep sources of contemporary democratic fragility. Citizens who have never truly practiced deliberation do not exercise it well when the stakes are serious.

The Gen_Home family council is a permanent practice room. Not a school simulation disconnected from real stakes, but a domestic institution where decisions have effective consequences. If Alexis loses the vote on his gaming time, he plays less — for real. If Marc fails to keep his commitment about the phone at dinner, everyone sees it — for real. If the proposal to rebalance household chores is adopted, it must be honored — for real. Kitchen stakes are not parliamentary stakes. But the structure of deliberation is identical: parties with divergent interests, a framework of shared rules, a legitimate decision-making process, mandatory consequences for losers and winners alike. The child who grows up in this framework develops an intuition for democracy that no civic education lesson can replicate as effectively.

What I am proposing here is not a technological product with secondary civic features. It is a thesis: that household technology, if designed with the right principles, can be a civic training institution more powerful than many institutions explicitly dedicated to that purpose. The family is the individual's first political space — the first place where they learn they are not alone, that their decisions affect others, that resources are finite and must be distributed, that rules can be unjust and deserve to be challenged through arguments. To equip this first political space with an infrastructure that takes it seriously — that preserves its memory, that makes its deliberations visible and its decisions traceable — is to bet on democracy at its deepest root.

In twenty years, Mathieu Vézina will be thirty-four. He may have children of his own. He will likely give them a family council, because that is how institutions are transmitted: not through obligation but through example, not through decree but through the intimate conviction that it is a good way to live together. He may not remember the technical details of the box that sat on the spice shelf in the Bellechasse kitchen. He may not be able to name the language model or the RAG architecture or model quantization. But he will know — in his bones, in his reflexes — that you can ask for help without losing your dignity, that you can change rules through argument, that you can be wrong without being diminished, and that a house that knows its inhabitants well can make them freer, not less. This is no small legacy. It may be the only one that truly matters.

The intelligence of the home is not a feature. It is an invitation to revisit the question of what technology is supposed to do for us. Not to make us more efficient in service of the economy. Not to connect us to more content than we can absorb. Not to offer us more choices than our brains can reasonably evaluate. But to help us be present in our lives, to our children, to our aging parents, to our neighbors, to our ideas, to everything that gives a life its meaning and texture. Sovereign technology is not countercultural. It is pro-human. And in a century that sorely needs it, this is a distinction worth defending.
The honest limits of the vision

It would be dishonest to end this act without naming the real frictions. Level 1 of Gen_Home — the software box — is feasible today, but it requires an initial technical configuration that the vast majority of families cannot do themselves. We must solve the accessibility problem without sacrificing sovereignty. The model of a certified technician who installs and configures on-site is a credible approach — but it requires a network of qualified technicians yet to be built. This is not an impossibility. It is a work in progress.

There is also the question of the entry price. A Beelink SER8 with 64 gigabytes of RAM costs, at the time of writing, between 700 and 900 Canadian dollars. Add installation time and a software license — and you have an initial investment representing several weeks of expenses for a median-income family. Two factors temper this concern: first, the price of computer hardware continues to fall — what costs 800 dollars in 2025 may cost 300 in 2028. Second, if Gen_Home effectively replaces several existing subscriptions — Netflix partially, Google One, Microsoft 365 — the return on investment can be positive within two to three years. But naming the obstacle clearly is necessary: democratization does not happen by magic. It is built through pricing choices, business models, and subsidies.

Digital sovereignty cannot be a class privilege. If Gen_Home remains a product for wealthy families, it reproduces exactly the digital divide it claims to heal — just with a different aesthetic. I bear the responsibility of solving the accessibility equation as seriously as the technical equation.
« The home is the first territory of freedom. What happens there belongs to those who live there. What we forgot to demand, we can demand again. » — David Berthelotte
